summary:
The Shijiahe culture was a late Neolithic culture of the middle Yangtze River valley in present-day Hubei Province, China, which emerged around 2600 BCE and persisted until approximately 2000 BCE. Named after the type site of Shijiahe in Tianmen city, the culture represents one of the most complex pre-Bronze Age societies in southern China, featuring large walled settlement sites, specialized craft production, and long-distance exchange networks.

The Shijiahe site itself covered several square kilometers and was encircled by earthen ramparts, indicating a settlement of considerable size and organizational capacity. Archaeological finds include fine pottery, jade artifacts, and numerous small terracotta figurines depicting human and animal forms. The culture developed out of the earlier [[event:1676|Qujialing culture]] tradition and maintained interactions with contemporary cultures across the Yangtze Basin. Its eventual decline around 2000 BCE coincided with climatic changes and the southward expansion of cultures from the Yellow River region.
